title: Calendula infused oil
subtitle: A base oil for skin preparations
excerpt: Dried calendula flowers soaked in olive oil at 60-65°C for 4 hours. A wound-supportive base oil used as a skin treatment on its own or as the base for salves, balms, and creams.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, calendula entry: the infused oil preparation and wound-supportive tradition. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), the calendula oil preparation for wounds and skin. the herbal reference on Calendulae flos, the traditional use for minor wounds, dry skin, and irritation.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly to 80 g herb / 400 ml oil. The same 4-hour soaking time applies.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The infused oil keeps 12 months in a dark glass bottle, sealed and stored in a cool dark place. A small amount of vitamin E oil added at bottling (1-2 drops per 100 ml) extends shelf life slightly.
recipe.temperatureNote: Hold the oil between 60 and 65°C throughout the soaking. Below 55°C the extraction is poor; above 70°C the volatile active compounds begin to degrade.

glossaryTerms.definition: A carrier oil that has been macerated with dried herb material so the oil-soluble constituents (triterpenes, flavonoids, carotenoids in calendula) dissolve into the oil. The base for salves, balms, and creams, and useful as a skin oil on its own.

content.text: is a deeper extraction than the salve in the anchor batch: oil only, no beeswax, so the final product is fluid and can be used directly as a skin oil, added to a bath, or mixed into a cream. It is the first thing to make when building a household herbal medicine cabinet. The ratio is 1 part dried flowers to 5 parts oil by weight, soaked at 60-65°C for 4 hours in a double boiler. The temperature is critical: a probe thermometer is the only reliable way to hold 60-65°C, and eyeballing the heat is the most common failure mode.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Asteraceae allergy. Calendula is in the daisy family. If you have a known ragweed, chamomile, or daisy allergy, patch-test first: apply a small amount to the inside of the forearm and wait 24-48 hours before wider use.

External use only. This is a topical skin oil, not for internal use.

Skin conditions that need medical attention. Infected skin (warm, spreading redness, pus, fever) needs medical treatment before applying any oil or salve.

items.prepNote: whole dried calendula flowers; make sure they are fully dry, any moisture causes the oil to go rancid
good-quality olive oil; light olive oil (not extra-virgin) or sunflower oil work equally well and have a more neutral smell

content.text: Set up a double boiler: a heatproof bowl over a saucepan of barely simmering water. Combine the dried calendula flowers and olive oil in the bowl. Clip the probe thermometer to the side of the bowl with its tip in the oil but not touching the bottom. Bring the oil to 60-65°C and hold it there for 4 hours. Adjust the water bath as needed. Stir the herbs once an hour. By hour 2, the oil should turn deep golden-amber and smell strongly of calendula. At hour 4, the colour should be richer still.

Take the bowl off the heat. Set a sieve over a measuring jug, line with muslin cloth folded double. Pour the warm oil through. Gather the corners of the muslin and press the spent flowers firmly (this recovers 5-10% more oil). Strain a second time through fresh muslin if the oil looks cloudy with plant particles. Cloudiness shortens shelf life. Decant into amber glass bottles using a funnel. Label with the oil, date, and ratio (1:5).

Apply a small amount (3-5 drops) to the affected area and massage in gently, twice daily. Use as a face oil, massage oil, or skin treatment as needed. the herbal reference supports traditional use for dry skin, minor wounds, minor burns, and sunburn. For use on broken skin (minor cuts, after the wound has closed): apply externally to the healed area, not to open flesh.
Amber glass bottles, sealed, in a cool dark cupboard. Keeps 12 months. If the oil smells rancid (sour or musty rather than herbal), discard; rancid oil is not safe to apply to skin.
When not to use this remedy
Known Asteraceae allergy without patch-testing first. Infected skin. Rancid oil.
